items.q: Is the Gemäldegalerie Berlin worth visiting?
items.a: Yes, especially if Museum Island crowds put you off. The Gemäldegalerie holds one of the strongest old master collections in Europe, including a hall of Rembrandts, two Vermeers, Caravaggio’s Amor Vincit Omnia and Bruegel’s Dutch Proverbs, and it is usually calmer than Museum Island. Its collection-to-crowd ratio is unusually strong.

items.q: How long do you need for the Gemäldegalerie?
items.a: Plan 90 minutes to two hours for a focused visit. The museum has 72 rooms along roughly two kilometres of galleries, so seeing everything takes half a day and most visitors burn out. A better plan is to pick around five target paintings, follow the numbered main halls in one loop, and let the rest be a bonus.

items.q: How much do Gemäldegalerie tickets cost?
items.a: As checked in July 2026, tickets cost 14 euros for adults and 7 euros reduced, with under 18s free. The Kulturforum day ticket costs 22 euros and covers the exhibitions of the neighbouring Kulturforum institutions on the same day. The museum recommends booking online to avoid waiting at the ticket counter.

items.q: Is the Gemäldegalerie free on Sundays?
items.a: No. Berlin’s Museumssonntag, the free first Sunday of the month, ran for the last time on 1 December 2024 and was discontinued when the city cut its culture budget. Under 18s are always free, and the Neue Nationalgalerie next door is free on the first Thursday evening of each month from 16:00 to 20:00.

items.q: Is the Gemäldegalerie open on Mondays?
items.a: No, it is closed on Mondays, like most Berlin state museums. It is open Tuesday to Sunday from 10:00 to 18:00. If your only museum window is a Monday, plan around the venues that do open, and check current holiday hours on the official site before you go.

items.q: What is the difference between the Gemäldegalerie and Museum Island?
items.a: Museum Island is a cluster of five museums for antiquities, 19th century art and archaeology, and it takes the crowds. The Gemäldegalerie at the Kulturforum is a single purpose-built gallery for European painting from the 13th to 18th century. If you want Rembrandt, Vermeer, Bruegel and Caravaggio in a usually calmer setting, the Gemäldegalerie is the right building.
